1: vayera el Yonah raah gedolah vayichar lo: 'But it displeased Jonah exceedingly, and he was angry.' The ironic wordplay is at its sharpest: in Jonah 1:2, Nineveh's ra'ah (wickedness/evil) had come up before YHWH; now YHWH's forgiveness of Nineveh is raah gedolah (great evil) to Jonah. The same evaluative word applied to the city's sin is now applied to Jonah's reaction to the city's salvation. Vayichar lo (he was angry, literally 'it burned for him') — the verb charah (H2734) will be used four times in Jonah 4 (vv1, 4, 9[×2]) building the chapter's emotional arc.
2: haloa zeh devari ad heyoti al admati: 'Is this not what I said when I was still in my country?' Jonah's prayer of complaint contains the book's theological heart: he quotes Exodus 34:6 — ki ata el channun verachum erech apayim verab chesed venicham al hara'ah — 'for I knew that you are a gracious God and merciful, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting from disaster.' The six-fold character formula from Exodus 34:6-7 (the most quoted verse in the OT) is here used as an accusation: Jonah's complaint is not ignorance of God's character but an objection to that character being extended to Assyria. He did not flee out of fear of Nineveh but out of a desire that they not repent and be saved.
3: veatta YHWH qach na et nafshi mimmeni: 'Therefore now, O LORD, please take my life from me.' Jonah's death-wish echoes Elijah's in 1 Kings 19:4 (qach nafshi — same phrase) — the prophet burned out and wanting to die after his mission. But the contexts differ: Elijah is exhausted after a great victory and terrified by Jezebel's threat; Jonah is angry because his mission succeeded in a way he did not want. Ki tov moti mechayai — 'for death is better for me than life.' This is the book's lowest point for Jonah: the prophet who has just experienced the miracle of Nineveh's repentance requests death rather than endorses it.
4: vaYHWH ha'tov charah lach: 'And the LORD said, Do you do well to be angry?' YHWH's response to Jonah's complaint is a single question: ha'tov charah lach — literally 'Is it good for you to burn/be angry?' The question is deceptively simple but theologically pointed: is your anger the right response? The word tov (good) introduces the book's second evaluative frame — the first was ra'ah (evil/bad), now tov (good). YHWH does not rebuke Jonah's anger outright but poses the question, allowing Jonah to answer it through his own subsequent behavior. The question is left unanswered at this point — Jonah simply walks out of the city.
5: vayetze Yonah min hair vayeshev miqqedem lair: 'Jonah went out of the city and sat to the east of the city.' Jonah, receiving no satisfying answer to his complaint, does what displaced prophets do: he waits. He makes a booth (sukkah, H5521) and sits in its shade to see what will happen to the city. The sukkah vocabulary evokes the Feast of Tabernacles/Booths — the harvest festival celebrating YHWH's provision in the wilderness and the nations coming to Jerusalem (Zechariah 14:16-19 connects this festival with Gentile nations, including, specifically, Egypt and Nineveh). Jonah under his sukkah at Nineveh is a bitterly ironic Tabernacles scene.
6: vayeman YHWH elohim qiqayon: 'Now the LORD God appointed a plant.' The same verb mana (appointed, H4487) as Jonah 1:17 (the fish). YHWH now appoints four objects: fish (Jonah 1:17), plant (Jonah 4:6), worm (Jonah 4:7), east wind (Jonah 4:8) — all four are instruments of his pedagogical purpose for Jonah. The qiqayon (H7021, usually translated gourd or castor oil plant — a large-leafed plant that provides shade quickly) grows over Jonah's head to give shade. Wayesamach Yonah al haqiqayon simcha gedolah — 'and Jonah was exceedingly glad because of the plant, with great joy.' The same adjectival construction as Jonah 1:16 (the sailors' great fear before YHWH) — Jonah's intense joy over the plant stands in ironic contrast to his reaction to Nineveh's salvation.
7: vayeman ha'elohim tola'at baalot hashachar lamochorat: 'But God appointed a worm when dawn came up the next day, and it attacked the plant, so that it withered.' The worm (tola'at, H8438) — ordinarily a symbol of death and decay (Job 24:20; Isaiah 14:11; Jonah 66:24) — is YHWH's instrument to remove Jonah's joy. Vatach (attacked/struck) — the same verb used of snakebite and scorpion sting. The plant that provided abundant shade is dried up by a single worm — the reversal of Jonah's brief comfort is as complete as it is sudden. This precision — a single worm, at dawn, the very next day — underscores that nothing in this narrative is accidental.
8: vayehi bizerot hashemesh vayeman elohim ruach qadim charish: 'When the sun rose, God appointed a scorching east wind.' The qadim charish (scorching east wind) is the sirocco — the hot, desiccating wind from the desert that can raise temperatures dramatically and sap a person's strength. Under this combined assault (sun and wind without shade), Jonah asks to die a second time: tov moti mechayai — 'death is better for me than life' (verbatim repetition of Jonah 4:3). The doubling of the death-wish marks the depth of his disorder: first he wanted to die because Nineveh was saved; now he wants to die because a plant died. The disproportionality is the book's indictment.
9: vaYHWH el Yonah ha'tov charah lach al haqiqayon: 'Then God said to Jonah, Do you do well to be angry for the plant?' YHWH repeats his question from v4 but specifies the object: the plant. Jonah's answer is unambiguous: heitev charah li ad mawet — 'I do well to be angry, angry enough to die.' The escalation from v4 (where Jonah had silently walked away) to v9 (where he asserts the rightness of his anger to the point of death) marks Jonah's refusal to be corrected. The book's final pedagogical moment is set: YHWH will use Jonah's own emotional investment in the plant to expose the incoherence of his position.
10: atta chasta al haqiqayon asher lo amalta bo: 'You pity the plant, for which you did not labor.' YHWH's argument moves from lesser to greater: you (atta, emphatic) had compassion (chasta, H2347 — to spare/take care of/pity) for a plant that you did not grow, tend, or cultivate, that came up in a night and perished in a night. The plant is YHWH's miniature case study for the principle of care without investment. The argument is not just rational but emotional: Jonah's emotional response to the plant's death reveals that he is capable of caring deeply about things — his care is just misdirected.
11: va'ani lo achusa al Nineveh hair hagedolah: 'And should I not pity Nineveh, that great city?' The book ends with a question — YHWH's unanswered rhetorical question to Jonah and, through the narrative, to every reader. Nineveh has harbeh mish-shteim esreh riboh adam — more than 120,000 persons who do not know their right hand from their left (asher lo yada bein yemino lismolo — the idiom for moral ignorance/infancy, not literally unable to distinguish directions) — plus much cattle (ubehema rabbah). The book's final word is rabbah (much/many/great) — the same adjective used of the great wind (Jonah 1:4), the great storm (Jonah 1:12), and Jonah's great joy at the plant (Jonah 4:6). YHWH's compassion for Nineveh is proportioned to its greatness and ignorance; Jonah's pitilessness is exposed as incoherent in light of his pity for a plant. The deliberate open ending invites the reader to answer for Jonah — and for themselves.
